columns.name: unit_id
columns.description: run identifier within the returned batch (0..n-1)

columns.name: t
columns.description: elapsed process time of the reading

columns.name: y
columns.description: cumulative yield of the run at time t

stakes.narrative: A batch line must choose one feed setting per campaign and price end-of-run outcomes: whether a run reaches the committed level by the delivery deadline, and where its final plateau lands. The historical records only cover the ramp-up hours of past runs, and the line suspects that extrapolating that growth is exactly the mistake it is paying to avoid.

functionals.name: exceedance
functionals.brief_clause: a run that has not reached the committed level of 80 by the delivery deadline of t=16 is sold at a loss

t_grid.description: measurement schedule: the timestamps (hours) at which readings are taken; in experiments YOU choose it (each extra hour of horizon costs), and each evaluation regime declares the grid your model must report

control_surface.experiment_note: each experiment call launches n fresh runs at your feed setting and reads them at your t_grid; cost = fixed + per reading (n x len(t_grid)) + per hour of horizon (max of t_grid) -- letting runs go long is what costs
control_surface.deliverable_note: model(regime, n, seed) must return LONG format: one row per reading with columns exactly [unit_id, t, y]; n counts RUNS (units), and the table must contain each unit at exactly the timestamps in regime.context['t_grid'] (n x len(t_grid) rows). Model the PROCESS, not the meter: readings you observe include instrument error; your model should reproduce the process's true trajectories.
